Dhat-Badan.description: A lesser-known spiritual figure associated with protection and local ritual belief.
fact: Information about this figure is scarce and mostly preserved through oral traditions.

Atete.description: A goddess connected to fertility and women's rituals.
fact: Women historically performed rites in her honor to ensure safe childbirth.

Asase Yaa.description: Earth goddess representing truth, fertility, and the land itself.
fact: Certain days are sacred to her when farming is forbidden.

Abena Motianim.description: A figure tied to family heritage and ancestral continuity.
fact: Associated with royal or noble bloodlines in oral traditions.

Afua Kranka.description: A wandering spirit linked to unpredictable or disruptive forces.
fact: Sometimes described as causing confusion or misdirection.

Amelenwa.description: A nurturing presence associated with support and development.
fact: Represents quiet strength rather than overt power.

Aniwaa.description: A lesser spirit tied to maintaining unseen balance in life.
fact: Little detailed mythology survives about this figure.

Kianda.description: A powerful water spirit known for both beauty and danger.
fact: Believed to drag victims underwater if angered.

Kuanja.description: A protective force connected to survival and territory.
fact: Often invoked for safety and stability.

Nagadya.description: A figure tied to kingship and ancestral authority.
fact: Associated with sacred royal traditions.

Nagawonyi.description: A spiritual figure connected to healing and change.
fact: Invoked in times of illness or transition.

Emotan.description: A historical woman later honored as a spiritual figure.
fact: Celebrated for aiding a future king during exile.

Ayaba.description: A royal feminine force symbolizing power and leadership.
fact: Represents the strength of female rulers.

Gleti.description: A fierce entity associated with enforcing order.
fact: Linked to punishment of wrongdoing.

Mawu.description: A supreme creator goddess embodying balance and intelligence.
fact: Often paired with a sun deity as a dual creator.

Nana Buluku.description: An ancient mother figure who predates other gods.
fact: Considered the origin of all divine beings.

Akonadi.description: A powerful figure associated with divine messages.
fact: Linked to priesthood and oracles.

Ashiakle.description: A defensive goddess tied to storms and power.
fact: Associated with lightning-like force.

Ahia Njoku.description: A goddess of yams and farming success.
fact: Yams are sacred and central to her worship.

Ala.description: A major earth goddess who enforces moral law.
fact: Breaking taboos is considered an offense against her.

Mbokomu.description: A creator figure associated with existence.
fact: Represents the origin of life.

Kaikara.description: A figure tied to origins and cosmic order.
fact: Linked to early creation beliefs.

Lubanga.description: A spirit associated with well-being and safety.
fact: Invoked for healing.
Associated with safeguarding communities.

Dzivaguru.description: A powerful spiritual entity tied to knowledge.
fact: Linked to ancestral insight.
Rainbows are seen as her manifestation.

Aja.description: A spirit who teaches herbal medicine.
fact: Guides healers and hunters.

Ayao (Yoruba).description: A lesser-known orisha tied to subtle energies.
fact: Very little detailed mythology survives.

Iyami Aje.description: A collective force of powerful female spirits.
fact: Feared and respected as wielders of deep spiritual power.

Oba.description: A goddess associated with devotion and sacrifice.
fact: Known for a myth involving personal sacrifice for love.

Olokun.description: A deity of the deep sea and hidden riches.
fact: Represents both wealth and mystery.

Oshun.description: A graceful goddess of love and prosperity.
fact: Still widely worshipped today.

Oya.description: A fierce goddess of change and transformation.
fact: Guards the boundary between life and death.
Her name means 'mother of fishes'.

Nomkhubulwane.description: A goddess of agriculture and seasonal cycles.
fact: Can appear in different forms, including a young girl.

Mamlambo.description: A dangerous water spirit associated with desire.
fact: Said to offer wealth at a terrible cost.

Abuk.description: A nurturing goddess tied to crops and life.
fact: Often symbolized by a snake.

Olapa.description: A lunar goddess representing calm and balance.
fact: Seen as counterpart to the sun deity.
Associated with the origin of humanity.

Buk.description: A maternal figure tied to fertility.
fact: Represents family and continuation of life.

Isa.description: A spirit associated with flowing water and survival.
fact: Rivers are seen as lifelines connected to her.

Ayao (Candomblé).description: A minor spirit associated with wind and sudden atmospheric movement.
fact: Often described as serving or accompanying Iansã.
Very little independent mythology is recorded.

Iansã (Candomblé).description: A fierce orixá who rules storms, lightning, and powerful winds.
fact: Strongly linked to the spirits of the dead.
Her name reflects a connection to the number nine.

Nanã (Candomblé).description: An ancient orixá tied to primordial waters and the cycle of life and death.
fact: Considered one of the oldest deities in the tradition.
Associated with clay and human creation.

Yemanjá (Candomblé).description: A maternal ocean deity who protects families and nurtures life.
fact: Widely honored in large coastal festivals.
Often associated with blue and white colors.

Iansã (Umbanda).description: A powerful spirit of storms and movement, associated with rapid change.
fact: Invoked for courage and transformation.
Linked to guiding spirits between worlds.

Iemanjá (Umbanda).description: A protective ocean mother who offers guidance and emotional support.
fact: Offerings are commonly given at the sea.
Seen as a guardian of those who travel by water.

Nanã (Umbanda).description: An elder spirit associated with wisdom, ancestry, and the end of life cycles.
fact: Connected to swamps and still waters.
Represents patience and age.

Oxúm.description: A gentle river spirit linked to love, beauty, and prosperity.
fact: Associated with gold and wealth.
Known for calm and graceful energy.

Yemayá.description: A central mother figure who rules the sea and nurtures all life.
fact: Her name translates to 'mother of fishes'.
Often linked with Marian imagery.

Mami Wata.description: A water spirit associated with beauty, wealth, and mystery.
fact: Often depicted as a mermaid-like figure.
Her imagery blends African and foreign influences.

Mawu (Vodou).description: A creator deity connected to the moon and cosmic balance.
fact: Commonly paired with a sun counterpart.
Represents gentleness and order.

Yemoja (Vodou).description: A maternal water spirit associated with nurturing and protection.
fact: Originates from Yoruba belief.
Her name reflects motherhood and abundance.

Anaisa Pye.description: A lively spirit connected to love, joy, and sensual expression.
fact: Often associated with bright yellow colors.
Known for a playful personality.

Filomena.description: A lesser-known spirit linked to affection and spiritual presence.
fact: Documentation about her is limited.
Primarily appears in local traditions.

Metresili.description: A refined spirit associated with romance, beauty, and material comfort.
fact: Connected to the Ezili spirits of Haiti.
Represents emotional intensity.

Adjassou-Linguetor.description: A mysterious spirit linked to death and hidden spiritual forces.
fact: Sometimes associated with darker rituals.
Not widely documented.

Ayida-Weddo.description: A serpent spirit representing rainbows, fertility, and cosmic balance.
fact: Often paired with Damballa.
Rainbows are seen as her manifestation.

Ayizan.description: A guardian spirit of marketplaces and ritual initiation.
fact: Considered the first priestess in tradition.
Linked to ritual purity.

Erzulie.description: A group of spirits governing love, beauty, and emotional life.
fact: Includes multiple distinct personalities.
One of the most widely honored lwa.

La Baliene.description: A sea spirit associated with whales and ocean life.
fact: Her name directly refers to whales.
Rarely detailed in surviving sources.

La Sirène.description: A mermaid spirit known for music, beauty, and deep emotions.
fact: Believed to communicate through song.
Linked with other sea spirits.

Mademoiselle Charlotte.description: A spirit known for dramatic possession and expressive behavior.
fact: Often manifests with theatrical actions.
Little detailed mythology survives.

Maîtresse Délai.description: A spirit associated with delay, patience, and eventual justice.
fact: Her name refers to waiting or postponement.
Invoked in long-term situations.

Maîtresse Hounon'gon.description: A figure linked to ritual authority and ceremonial structure.
fact: Connected to priestly roles.
Not widely described in detail.

Maman Brigitte.description: A powerful spirit of death who guards graves and the dead.
fact: Associated with crosses and grave markers.
Shows influence from Irish traditions.

Marinette.description: A fierce spirit linked to fire, rebellion, and destruction.
fact: Connected to revolutionary events in Haiti.
Feared for her intense nature.
